**Geographical distance** or **geodetic distance** is the [distance](/wiki/Distance "Distance") measured along the surface of the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth"), or the shortest arc length.

The formulae in this article calculate distances between points which are defined by [geographical coordinates](/wiki/Geographical_coordinates "Geographical coordinates") in terms of [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") and [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude"). This distance is an element in solving the [second (inverse) geodetic problem](/wiki/Geodesy#Geodetic_problems "Geodesy").

## Introduction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographical_distance&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Introduction")]

Calculating the distance between geographical coordinates is based on some level of abstraction; it does not provide an *exact* distance, which is unattainable if one attempted to account for every irregularity in the surface of the Earth.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Common abstractions for the surface between two geographic points are:

* Flat surface;
* Spherical surface;
* Ellipsoidal surface.

All abstractions above ignore changes in elevation. Calculation of distances which account for changes in elevation relative to the idealized surface are not discussed in this article.

### Classification of Formulae based on Approximation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographical_distance&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Classification of Formulae based on Approximation")]

* short-range approximations: Flat surface, Gauss-mid-latitude; ![{\displaystyle \max |\Delta D_{\text{error}}|\propto D^{3}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f9a7cd6725678fe364f8d11952b24152afeab5a6)
  + Bowring's method (1981) for short lines improved by Karney using reduces latitude and mid-latitude; ![{\displaystyle \max |\Delta D_{\text{error}}|\propto D^{4}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9755f4a37a174783ca6329e2d35cf1c75ff9400f)
* long-range approximations; ![{\displaystyle \max |\Delta D_{\text{error}}|\propto D}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/3f511ebe3ebe3be057d119d0a8efebd97959fce2) on the closed hemisphere
  + ![{\displaystyle f^{0}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1a110dcc3d54a21f3d6bce5d797dc3afbcda9c43)-order approximation method: Spherical surface
  + higher-order approximations based on Ellipsoid: ![{\displaystyle f^{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/be4f3fefdf58c518ef2ab0eb0f8af568048fa303): Andoyer(1932); Andoyer-Lambert(1942), ![{\displaystyle f^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/81efa211d9d04493b68b24bf9843d1967ae22cdc): Andoyer-Lambert-Thomas(1970), ![{\displaystyle f^{3}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6c9494b7badb4e14f031ca84e8ced9cecb0bc919): Vincenty(1975), ![{\displaystyle f^{6}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a2339163210c976d41992e59b6024308550c649c): Karney(2011)

The theoretical estimations of error are added in above and ![{\displaystyle f}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/132e57acb643253e7810ee9702d9581f159a1c61) is the [flattening](/wiki/Flattening "Flattening") of the Earth.

### Nomenclature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographical_distance&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Nomenclature")]

Arc distance, ![{\displaystyle D,\,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1dd9d673660156691519e4140ab1396b1d6fb434) is the minimum distance along the surface of sphere/ellipsoid calculated between two points, ![{\displaystyle P_{1}\,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7e013a98ef29ddc6b3fbe06ca9782a3e9bc75c54) and ![{\displaystyle P_{2}\,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/33ea878e73d506f3f39333c37acdcd785ce6cd99). Whereas, the tunnel distance, or chord length, ![{\displaystyle D_{\textrm {t}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/67c1561bb59f11f7de84bffa5ecc1fdc8fca2804), is measured along Cartesian straight line. The geographical coordinates of the two points, as (latitude, longitude) pairs, are ![{\displaystyle (\phi _{1},\lambda _{1})\,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/70fef3d9ea82107c4b2baab3623a7bfce108019b) and ![{\displaystyle (\phi _{2},\lambda _{2}),\,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/848466d9d449bd795821b883aa3c6b81ed1417ac) respectively. Which of the two points is designated as ![{\displaystyle P_{1}\,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7e013a98ef29ddc6b3fbe06ca9782a3e9bc75c54) is not important for the calculation of distance.

Latitude ![{\displaystyle \phi \,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/30ae608cf0ade0577802bc4ffa026a29ab35c365) and longitude ![{\displaystyle \lambda \,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cd9a9699fc8f22e730ed70d555b16e9e164b386c) coordinates on maps are usually expressed in [degrees](/wiki/Degree_(angle) "Degree (angle)"). In the given forms of the formulae below, one or more values *must* be expressed in the specified units to obtain the correct result. Where geographic coordinates are used as the argument of a trigonometric function, the values may be expressed in any angular units compatible with the method used to determine the value of the trigonometric function. Many electronic calculators allow calculations of trigonometric functions in either degrees or [radians](/wiki/Radian "Radian"). The calculator mode must be compatible with the units used for geometric coordinates.

Differences in latitude and longitude are labeled and calculated as follows:

:   ![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}\Delta \phi &=\phi _{2}-\phi _{1};\\\Delta \lambda &=\lambda _{2}-\lambda _{1}.\end{aligned}}\,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e46b6d5c687ad08b29603f8d1f21ffd597a117b7)

It is not important whether the result is positive or negative when used in the formulae below.

"Mid-latitude" is labeled and calculated as follows:

:   ![{\displaystyle \phi _{\mathrm {m} }={\frac {\phi _{1}+\phi _{2}}{2}}.\,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7eced0786a9c696d25769c21863a66d194091191)

Unless specified otherwise, the [radius](/wiki/Earth_radius "Earth radius") of the Earth for the calculations below is:

:   ![{\displaystyle R\,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/eb2e17aa72cfae5654cafa7ec1ef361da9da1bd0) = 6,371.009 kilometers = 3,958.761 statute miles = 3,440.069 [nautical miles](/wiki/Nautical_mile "Nautical mile").

![{\displaystyle D_{\,}\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e8ad985205df59d1e8de2fb2ffbc0910e81778fb) = Distance between the two points, as measured along the surface of the Earth and in the same units as the value used for radius unless specified otherwise.

### Singularities and discontinuity of latitude/longitude

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographical_distance&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Singularities and discontinuity of latitude/longitude")]

The approximation of sinusoidal functions of ![{\displaystyle \Delta \lambda }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/905140cc6559a946172b382a0da12bbc82096801), appearing in some flat-surface formulae below, may induce singularity and discontinuity. It may also degrade the accuracy in the case of higher latitude.

Longitude has [singularities](/wiki/Mathematical_singularity "Mathematical singularity") at the [Poles](/wiki/Geographical_pole "Geographical pole") (longitude is undefined) and a [discontinuity](/wiki/Discontinuity_(mathematics) "Discontinuity (mathematics)") at the ±[180° meridian](/wiki/180th_meridian "180th meridian"). Also, planar projections of the [circles of constant latitude](/wiki/Circle_of_latitude "Circle of latitude") are highly curved near the Poles. Hence, the above equations for [delta](/wiki/Delta_(letter) "Delta (letter)") latitude/longitude (![{\displaystyle \Delta \phi \!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/39276505d51b8f419c267e4461faf4017a3a717a), ![{\displaystyle \Delta \lambda \!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/89cf8ad0d0c41134b627bc670a8b80bacaede4f3)) and mid-latitude (![{\displaystyle \phi _{\mathrm {m} }\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/92d77a7b93f4a9197ec2afbb96a49c7fef601ce2)) may not give the expected answer for positions near the Poles or the ±180° meridian. Consider e.g. the value of ![{\displaystyle \Delta \lambda \!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/89cf8ad0d0c41134b627bc670a8b80bacaede4f3) ("east displacement") when ![{\displaystyle \lambda _{1}\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4f7fec2c795835b7db4f4fcb1b36e59617daebd7) and ![{\displaystyle \lambda _{2}\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/31a0e884dd753c29f96c50fa56427740d674c1a6) are on either side of the ±180° meridian, or the value of ![{\displaystyle \phi _{\mathrm {m} }\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/92d77a7b93f4a9197ec2afbb96a49c7fef601ce2) ("mid-latitude") for the two positions (![{\displaystyle \phi _{1}\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ad3f60053da99982c0b4a33221d7195df6afd3ae)=89°, ![{\displaystyle \lambda _{1}\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4f7fec2c795835b7db4f4fcb1b36e59617daebd7)=45°) and (![{\displaystyle \phi _{2}\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9d0f8684ebac824b74bd03d208d017234a291c3b)=89°, ![{\displaystyle \lambda _{2}\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/31a0e884dd753c29f96c50fa56427740d674c1a6)=−135°).

If a calculation based on latitude/longitude should be valid for all Earth positions, it should be verified that the discontinuity and the Poles are handled correctly. Another solution is to use [*n*-vector](/wiki/N-vector "N-vector") instead of latitude/longitude, since this [representation](/wiki/Horizontal_position_representation "Horizontal position representation") does not have discontinuities or singularities.

## Flat-surface approximation formulae for very short distance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographical_distance&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Flat-surface approximation formulae for very short distance")]

A planar approximation for the surface of the Earth may be useful over very small distances. It approximates the arc length, ![{\displaystyle D}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f34a0c600395e5d4345287e21fb26efd386990e6), to the tunnel distance, ![{\displaystyle D_{\textrm {t}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/67c1561bb59f11f7de84bffa5ecc1fdc8fca2804), or omits the conversion between arc and chord lengths shown below.

The shortest distance between two points in plane is a Cartesian straight line. The [Pythagorean theorem](/wiki/Pythagorean_theorem "Pythagorean theorem") is used to calculate the distance between points in a plane.

Even over short distances, the accuracy of geographic distance calculations which assume a flat Earth depend on the method by which the latitude and longitude coordinates have been [projected](/wiki/Map_projection "Map projection") onto the plane. The projection of latitude and longitude coordinates onto a plane is the realm of [cartography](/wiki/Cartography "Cartography").

The formulae presented in this section provide varying degrees of accuracy.

### Spherical Earth approximation formulae

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographical_distance&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Spherical Earth approximation formulae")]

The [tunnel distance](#Tunnel_distance), ![{\displaystyle D_{\textrm {t}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/67c1561bb59f11f7de84bffa5ecc1fdc8fca2804), is calculated on Spherical Earth. This formula takes into account the variation in distance between meridians with latitude, assuming ![{\displaystyle D\approx D_{\textrm {t}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/03f92043f8cbccbf33e4bb8c3d26782e06792fc1):

:   ![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}D_{\textrm {t}}&=2R{\sqrt {\left(\sin {\frac {\Delta \phi }{2}}\,\cos {\frac {\Delta \lambda }{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\cos \phi _{\textrm {m}}\sin {\frac {\Delta \lambda }{2}}\right)^{2}}}\\&\approx R{\sqrt {\left(\Delta \phi \,\cos {\frac {\Delta \lambda }{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(2\cos \phi _{\textrm {m}}\sin {\frac {\Delta \lambda }{2}}\right)^{2}}}\ .\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e937cad1260979ef2f1acce9b8a1f65225debe14)

The square root appearing above can be eliminated for such applications as ordering locations by distance in a database query. On the other hand, some methods for computing nearest neighbors, such as the [vantage-point tree](/wiki/Vantage-point_tree "Vantage-point tree"), require that the distance metric obey the [triangle inequality](/wiki/Triangle_inequality "Triangle inequality"), in which case the square root must be retained.

#### In the case of medium or low latitude

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographical_distance&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: In the case of medium or low latitude")]

Although not being universal, the above is furthermore simplified by approximating sinusoidal functions of ![{\displaystyle {\frac {\Delta \lambda }{2}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4a376ff54d057987d680da74e5d3470fd83293b8), justified except for high latitude:

:   ![{\displaystyle D\approx R{\sqrt {(\Delta \phi )^{2}+(\cos(\phi _{\mathrm {m} })\Delta \lambda )^{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/433abbbca7dd2b4218ca76f40341b8c805d8e90f).

### Ellipsoidal Earth approximation formulae

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographical_distance&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Ellipsoidal Earth approximation formulae")]

The above formula is extended for ellipsoidal Earth:

:   ![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}D&\approx 2{\sqrt {\left(M\left(\phi _{\textrm {m}}\right)\sin {\frac {\Delta \phi }{2}}\,\cos {\frac {\Delta \lambda }{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(N\left(\phi _{\textrm {m}}\right)\cos \phi _{\textrm {m}}\sin {\frac {\Delta \lambda }{2}}\right)^{2}}},\\&\approx {\sqrt {\left(M\left(\phi _{\textrm {m}}\right)\Delta \phi \,\cos {\frac {\Delta \lambda }{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(2N\left(\phi _{\textrm {m}}\right)\cos \phi _{\textrm {m}}\sin {\frac {\Delta \lambda }{2}}\right)^{2}}},\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/034cfd3815af2f55929de0c4e6d9d7b87a83907f)

where ![{\displaystyle M\,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/38ab25f751745f662bc57392d83cd4287df34010) and ![{\displaystyle N\,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ddb851f080941b78bd68dd1716dee13e010e1595) are the ***m**eridional* and its perpendicular, or "***n**ormal*", [radii of curvature of Earth](/wiki/Earth_radius#Principal_radii_of_curvature "Earth radius") (See also "[Geographic coordinate conversion](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_conversion#Orthogonality "Geographic coordinate conversion")" for their formulas).

It is derived by the approximation of ![{\displaystyle \left(\cos \phi _{\textrm {m}}\sin {\frac {\Delta \lambda }{2}}\Delta \phi \right)^{2}\approx 0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d6ff313a52d143397f3ade28df03207652f06724) in the square root.

This approximation can be viewed simply as the 3D Cartesian chord distance between two points on the ellipsoid, and equivalently as a chordal simplification of the Gauss mid-latitude method. Although we have not found this explicit formula in classical sources, the Gauss mid-latitude method itself is described in Rapp (1991).

#### In the case of medium or low latitude

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographical_distance&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: In the case of medium or low latitude")]

Although not being universal, the above is furthermore simplified by approximating sinusoidal functions of ![{\displaystyle {\frac {\Delta \lambda }{2}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4a376ff54d057987d680da74e5d3470fd83293b8), justified except for high latitude as above:[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

:   ![{\displaystyle D\approx {\sqrt {(M(\phi _{\mathrm {m} })\Delta \phi )^{2}+(N(\phi _{\mathrm {m} })\cos \phi _{\mathrm {m} }\Delta \lambda )^{2}}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9d142797f6a4d4a04034bc5c96bb0c50ca0fe955)

#### FCC's formula

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographical_distance&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: FCC's formula")]

The [Federal Communications Commission](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission") (FCC) prescribes the following formulae for distances not exceeding 475 kilometres (295 mi):[[4]](#cite_note-4)

:   ![{\displaystyle D\approx {\sqrt {(K_{1}\Delta \phi )^{2}+(K_{2}\Delta \lambda )^{2}}},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5ae2bd43d523487b04e958334680ddf5bf79be2a)
:   where

    :   ![{\displaystyle D\,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6389218f226c4cab8def75a7c16e52ff52e467d4) = Distance in kilometers;
    :   ![{\displaystyle \Delta \phi \,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fa6c0a8cd81f28c29e188e8417c701628606b7fd) and ![{\displaystyle \Delta \lambda \,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/31e7a7db05c0d4305ef87604f65c5fdcb9f0308c) are in degrees;
    :   ![{\displaystyle \phi _{\mathrm {m} }\,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8292f4361accf48ee472835330ba106b317e2ce7) must be in units compatible with the method used for determining ![{\displaystyle \cos \phi _{\mathrm {m} };\,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cbb267b01a378c5388e6e013c413a3f774ea61e4)
    :   ![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}K_{1}&=111.13209-0.56605\cos(2\phi _{\mathrm {m} })+0.00120\cos(4\phi _{\mathrm {m} });\\K_{2}&=111.41513\cos(\phi _{\mathrm {m} })-0.09455\cos(3\phi _{\mathrm {m} })+0.00012\cos(5\phi _{\mathrm {m} }).\end{aligned}}\,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2bf7718a2b98bee8e950475a2f87185ca8341af7)

:   Where ![{\displaystyle K_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8520077dbcf03c2aabefd98d41a2269ed41a54fa) and ![{\displaystyle K_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/57e1b324cf5b68f2729a8634ff76e396b634b75d) are in units of kilometers per arc degree. They are derived from [radii of curvature of Earth](/wiki/Earth_radius#Principal_radii_of_curvature "Earth radius") as follows:

    :   ![{\displaystyle K_{1}=M(\phi _{\mathrm {m} }){\frac {\pi }{180}}\,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/265c5d060df57ea7525dfb3c34f96b1728f76b2f) = kilometers per arc degree of latitude difference;
    :   ![{\displaystyle K_{2}=\cos(\phi _{\mathrm {m} })N(\phi _{\mathrm {m} }){\frac {\pi }{180}}\,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5af5f111d7b918b00292e5c0b023b7ed9e365e5a) = kilometers per arc degree of longitude difference;
:   Note that the expressions in the FCC formula are derived from the truncation of the [binomial series](/wiki/Binomial_series "Binomial series") expansion form of ![{\displaystyle M\,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/38ab25f751745f662bc57392d83cd4287df34010) and ![{\displaystyle N\,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ddb851f080941b78bd68dd1716dee13e010e1595), set to the *Clarke 1866* [reference ellipsoid](/wiki/Reference_ellipsoid "Reference ellipsoid"). For a more computationally efficient implementation of the formula above, multiple applications of cosine can be replaced with a single application and use of recurrence relation for [Chebyshev polynomials](/wiki/Chebyshev_polynomials "Chebyshev polynomials").

### Polar coordinate flat-Earth formula

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographical_distance&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Polar coordinate flat-Earth formula")]

![{\displaystyle D=R{\sqrt {\theta _{1}^{2}\;{\boldsymbol {+}}\;\theta _{2}^{2}\;\mathbf {-} \;2\theta _{1}\theta _{2}\cos(\Delta \lambda )}},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9e333c1e76b605ec2d53223fe6d14eb2c70e015a)

:   where the colatitude values are in radians: ![{\displaystyle \theta ={\frac {\pi }{2}}-\phi .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/3e91ddfe1b058b389e07138124c017aecb7bb4de)
:   For a latitude measured in degrees, the colatitude in radians may be calculated as follows: ![{\displaystyle \theta ={\frac {\pi }{180}}(90^{\circ }-\phi ).\,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f0c4c7e7183eace030be689b1fffbe5f4e326d82)

## Spherical-surface formulae

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographical_distance&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Spherical-surface formulae")]

Main article: [Great-circle distance](/wiki/Great-circle_distance "Great-circle distance")

If one is willing to accept a possible error of 0.5%, one can use formulas of [spherical trigonometry](/wiki/Spherical_trigonometry "Spherical trigonometry") on the sphere that best approximates the surface of the Earth.

The shortest distance along the surface of a sphere between two points on the surface is along the great-circle which contains the two points.

The [great-circle distance](/wiki/Great-circle_distance "Great-circle distance") article gives the formula for calculating the shortest arch length ![{\displaystyle D}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f34a0c600395e5d4345287e21fb26efd386990e6) on a sphere about the size of the Earth. That article includes an example of the calculation. For example, from **tunnel distance** ![{\displaystyle D_{\textrm {t}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/67c1561bb59f11f7de84bffa5ecc1fdc8fca2804),

:   ![{\displaystyle D=2R\arcsin {\frac {D_{\textrm {t}}}{2R}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2ab95f366ca3feece2dfba9fde7e468c22ab56b5)

For short distances (![{\displaystyle D\ll R}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d22427206257679385165452fa8cc205ac94a875)),

:   ![{\displaystyle D=D_{\textrm {t}}\left(1+{\frac {1}{24}}\left({\frac {D_{\textrm {t}}}{R}}\right)^{2}+\cdots \right).}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8c870be0e31f65bb1f36762f04c086361387c373)

### Tunnel distance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographical_distance&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Tunnel distance")]

A tunnel between points on Earth is defined by a Cartesian line through three-dimensional space between the points of interest.
The tunnel distance ![{\displaystyle D_{\textrm {t}}=2R\sin {\frac {D}{2R}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/30c7df95a60a9fba803c83e5b93cc453c05108e8) is the great-circle chord length and may be calculated as follows for the corresponding unit sphere:

:   ![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}\Delta {X}&=\cos(\phi _{2})\cos(\lambda _{2})-\cos(\phi _{1})\cos(\lambda _{1});\\\Delta {Y}&=\cos(\phi _{2})\sin(\lambda _{2})-\cos(\phi _{1})\sin(\lambda _{1});\\\Delta {Z}&=\sin(\phi _{2})-\sin(\phi _{1});\\D_{\textrm {t}}&=R{\sqrt {(\Delta {X})^{2}+(\Delta {Y})^{2}+(\Delta {Z})^{2}}}\\&=2R{\sqrt {\sin ^{2}{\frac {\Delta \phi }{2}}+\left(\cos ^{2}{\frac {\Delta \phi }{2}}-\sin ^{2}\phi _{\textrm {m}}\right)\sin ^{2}{\frac {\Delta \lambda }{2}}}}\\&=2R{\sqrt {\left(\sin {\frac {\Delta \lambda }{2}}\cos \phi _{\textrm {m}}\right)^{2}+\left(\cos {\frac {\Delta \lambda }{2}}\sin {\frac {\Delta \phi }{2}}\right)^{2}}}.\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f7a861b19e2a59097524d6e54f794b4c250b264d)

## Ellipsoidal-surface formulae

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographical_distance&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Ellipsoidal-surface formulae")]

Main article: [Geodesics on an ellipsoid](/wiki/Geodesics_on_an_ellipsoid "Geodesics on an ellipsoid")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Long_geodesic_on_an_oblate_ellipsoid.svg/250px-Long_geodesic_on_an_oblate_ellipsoid.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Long_geodesic_on_an_oblate_ellipsoid.svg)

Geodesic on an oblate ellipsoid

An ellipsoid approximates the surface of the Earth much better than a
sphere or a flat surface does. The shortest distance along the surface
of an ellipsoid between two points on the surface is along the
[geodesic](/wiki/Geodesic "Geodesic"). Geodesics follow more complicated paths than great
circles and in particular, they usually don't return to their starting
positions after one circuit of the Earth. This is illustrated in the
figure on the right where *f* is taken to be 1/50 to accentuate the
effect. Finding the geodesic between two points on the Earth, the
so-called [inverse geodetic problem](/wiki/Inverse_geodetic_problem "Inverse geodetic problem"), was the focus of many
mathematicians and geodesists over the course of the 18th and 19th
centuries with major contributions by
[Clairaut](/wiki/Alexis_Claude_Clairaut "Alexis Claude Clairaut"),[[5]](#cite_note-5)
[Legendre](/wiki/Adrien-Marie_Legendre "Adrien-Marie Legendre"),[[6]](#cite_note-6)
[Bessel](/wiki/Friedrich_Bessel "Friedrich Bessel"),[[7]](#cite_note-7)
and [Helmert](/wiki/Friedrich_Robert_Helmert "Friedrich Robert Helmert") English translation of [*Astron. Nachr.* **4**, 241–254 (1825)](http://adsabs.harvard.edu/full/1825AN......4..241B). [Errata](https://geographiclib.sourceforge.io/bessel-errata.html).[[8]](#cite_note-8)
Rapp[[9]](#cite_note-9)
provides a good summary of this work.

Methods for computing the geodesic distance are widely available in
[geographical information systems](/wiki/Geographical_information_systems "Geographical information systems"), software libraries, standalone
utilities, and online tools. The most widely used algorithm is by
[Vincenty](/wiki/Thaddeus_Vincenty "Thaddeus Vincenty"),[[10]](#cite_note-10)
who uses a series which is accurate to third order in the flattening of
the ellipsoid, i.e., about 0.5 mm; however, the algorithm fails to
converge for points that are nearly [antipodal](/wiki/Antipodes "Antipodes"). (For
details, see [Vincenty's formulae](/wiki/Vincenty%27s_formulae "Vincenty's formulae").) This defect is cured in the
algorithm given by
Karney,[[11]](#cite_note-11)
who employs series which are accurate to sixth order in the flattening.
This results in an algorithm which is accurate to full double precision
and which converges for arbitrary pairs of points on the Earth. This
algorithm is implemented in GeographicLib.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The exact methods above are feasible when carrying out calculations on a
computer. They are intended to give millimeter accuracy on lines of any
length; one can use simpler formulas if one doesn't need millimeter
accuracy, or if one does need millimeter accuracy but the line is short.

The short-line methods have been studied by several researchers.
Rapp,[[13]](#cite_note-rapp91-13) Chap. 6, describes the [Puissant](/wiki/Louis_Puissant "Louis Puissant") method,
the Gauss mid-latitude method, and the Bowring method.[[14]](#cite_note-bowring81-14) Karl Hubeny[[15]](#cite_note-15) got the expanded series of Gauss mid-latitude one represented as the correction to flat-surface one.

### Andoyer-Lambert formula for long lines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographical_distance&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Andoyer-Lambert formula for long lines")]

Historically, the long-line formulae were derived in the form of expansion series with regard to [flattening](/wiki/Flattening "Flattening") ![{\displaystyle f}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/132e57acb643253e7810ee9702d9581f159a1c61).[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Andoyer-Lambert formulae[[18]](#cite_note-18)
use the first-order correction (Andoyer, 1932) and [reduced latitude](/wiki/Latitude#Parametric_(or_reduced)_latitude "Latitude"), ![{\displaystyle \beta =\arctan \left((1-f)\tan \phi \right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/286067e96a9f5b0f87876520e5b688efb30106f5), for better accuracy. They give accuracy on the order of 10 meters over thousands of kilometers.

First convert the latitudes ![{\displaystyle \scriptstyle \phi _{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e62013385c90825acd4f56c916ce00943259fb52), ![{\displaystyle \scriptstyle \phi _{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e9376da39faf4bd7ae7351c30d13fa6e02c1be04) of the two points to [reduced latitudes](/wiki/Latitude#Parametric_(or_reduced)_latitude "Latitude") ![{\displaystyle \scriptstyle \beta _{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/54973ce2530d458b623a6d155182922af7a60003), ![{\displaystyle \scriptstyle \beta _{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d76e5a840865948a1eba143b5c60405c08ae97dd).
Then calculate the [central angle](/wiki/Central_angle "Central angle") ![{\displaystyle \sigma }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/59f59b7c3e6fdb1d0365a494b81fb9a696138c36) in radians between two points ![{\displaystyle (\beta _{1},\;\lambda _{1})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b70b0e788e1ffb1e2cac284b4e31928b4b61fdf9) and ![{\displaystyle (\beta _{2},\;\lambda _{2})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/867bc3dc5a409d22d86d466be7d66b945a3ec895) on a sphere using [the Great-circle distance method](/wiki/Great-circle_distance "Great-circle distance") ([haversine formula](/wiki/Haversine_formula "Haversine formula")), with longitudes ![{\displaystyle \lambda _{1}\;}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/012c738991054bf46f92c4c749e28a015a93ba42) and ![{\displaystyle \lambda _{2}\;}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/770ae82cb8ae2d0b3a8d2cb0243f802d92619e33) being the same on the sphere as on the spheroid.

:   ![{\displaystyle P={\frac {\beta _{1}+\beta _{2}}{2}}\qquad Q={\frac {\beta _{2}-\beta _{1}}{2}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/bf05b9c58f190898c415bd949d08b276d2dfb3be)

:   ![{\displaystyle X=(\sigma -\sin \sigma ){\frac {\sin ^{2}P\cos ^{2}Q}{\cos ^{2}{\frac {\sigma }{2}}}}\qquad \qquad Y=(\sigma +\sin \sigma ){\frac {\cos ^{2}P\sin ^{2}Q}{\sin ^{2}{\frac {\sigma }{2}}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a71e6dbeb465171c8c8e75648a7f231481e91c27)

:   ![{\textstyle D=a{\bigl (}\sigma -{\tfrac {f}{2}}(X+Y){\bigr )}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/83f836a2706c9b898e13f98bf845c6822c872029),

where ![{\displaystyle a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ffd2487510aa438433a2579450ab2b3d557e5edc) is the equatorial radius of the chosen spheroid.

On the [GRS 80](/wiki/GRS_80 "GRS 80") spheroid Lambert's formula is off by

:   0 North 0 West to 40 North 120 West, 12.6 meters
:   0N 0W to 40N 60W, 6.6 meters
:   40N 0W to 40N 60W, 0.85 meter

### Gauss mid-latitude method for short lines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographical_distance&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Gauss mid-latitude method for short lines")]

It has the similar form of the arc length converted from tunnel distance. Detailed formulas are given by Rapp,[[13]](#cite_note-rapp91-13) §6.4. It is consistent with the above-mentioned flat-surface formulae apparently.

:   ![{\displaystyle D=2N\left(\phi _{\textrm {m}}\right)\arcsin {\sqrt {\left(\sin {\frac {\Delta \lambda }{2}}\cos \phi _{\textrm {m}}\right)^{2}+\left(\cos {\frac {\Delta \lambda }{2}}\sin \left({\frac {\Delta \phi }{2}}{\frac {M\left(\phi _{\textrm {m}}\right)}{N\left(\phi _{\textrm {m}}\right)}}\right)\right)^{2}}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/bdf28a4b696135230c2de033c152d04620a2d380)

### Bowring's method for short lines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographical_distance&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Bowring's method for short lines")]

Bowring maps the points to a sphere of radius *R′*, with latitude and longitude represented as φ′ and λ′. Define

:   ![{\displaystyle A={\sqrt {1+e'^{2}\cos ^{4}\phi _{1}}},\quad B={\sqrt {1+e'^{2}\cos ^{2}\phi _{1}}},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6b6fc92e25bfbcb87d76aa197cb8218f1d120fdf)

where the second eccentricity squared is

:   ![{\displaystyle e'^{2}={\frac {a^{2}-b^{2}}{b^{2}}}={\frac {f(2-f)}{(1-f)^{2}}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/70e1ff64b419209b3b3338807dcc46ae581c2a1a)

The spherical radius is

:   ![{\displaystyle R'={\frac {\sqrt {1+e'^{2}}}{B^{2}}}a.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1764111a8eb2c0b3a22316a6d8087b70a822a911)

(The [Gaussian curvature](/wiki/Gaussian_curvature "Gaussian curvature") of the ellipsoid at φ1 is 1/*R′*2.)
The spherical coordinates are given by

:   ![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}\tan \phi _{1}'&={\frac {\tan \phi _{1}}{B}},\\\Delta \phi '&={\frac {\Delta \phi }{B}}{\biggl [}1+{\frac {3e'^{2}}{4B^{2}}}(\Delta \phi )\sin(2\phi _{1}+{\tfrac {2}{3}}\Delta \phi ){\biggr ]},\\\Delta \lambda '&=A\Delta \lambda ,\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/acee78ead1818a6414b8442884ef1fc4f387b8cf)

where ![{\displaystyle \Delta \phi =\phi _{2}-\phi _{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9dd3b920d2ba686e5ebd11d1896528802f25b6e5), ![{\displaystyle \Delta \phi '=\phi _{2}'-\phi _{1}'}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c6c02b6677374e53dd332ff15d339ad793734187),
![{\displaystyle \Delta \lambda =\lambda _{2}-\lambda _{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/27819db39701b9fb75d23687f95c3b432b55bece), ![{\displaystyle \Delta \lambda '=\lambda _{2}'-\lambda _{1}'}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5cb8b193e3c7c2a1193e03c2408eacabcd539bd5). The resulting problem on the sphere may be solved using the techniques for [great-circle navigation](/wiki/Great-circle_navigation "Great-circle navigation") to give approximations for the spheroidal distance and bearing. Detailed formulas are given by Rapp[[13]](#cite_note-rapp91-13) §6.5 and Bowring.[[14]](#cite_note-bowring81-14) The use of mid-latitude, ![{\displaystyle \phi _{\textrm {m}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4f822d06cd151dacd1cc48f8a0c1271441b8b9ed), improves the accuracy, shown by Karney.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Altitude correction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographical_distance&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Altitude correction")]

The variation in altitude from the topographical or ground level down to the sphere's or ellipsoid's surface, also changes the scale of distance measurements.[[20]](#cite_note-20)
The slant distance *s* ([chord](/wiki/Chord_(geometry) "Chord (geometry)") length) between two points can be reduced to the [arc length](/wiki/Arc_length "Arc length") on the ellipsoid surface *S* as:[[21]](#cite_note-T&G-21)

:   ![{\displaystyle S-s=-0.5(h_{1}+h_{2})s/R-0.5(h_{1}-h_{2})^{2}/s}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7cf916c0242846c06a2b258f39188bec451e7d7d)

where *R* is evaluated from Earth's [azimuthal radius of curvature](/wiki/Azimuthal_radius_of_curvature "Azimuthal radius of curvature") and *h* are [ellipsoidal heights](/wiki/Ellipsoidal_height "Ellipsoidal height") are each point.
The first term on the right-hand side of the equation accounts for the mean elevation and the second term for the inclination.
A further reduction of the above [Earth normal section](/wiki/Earth_normal_section "Earth normal section") length to the [ellipsoidal geodesic](/wiki/Ellipsoidal_geodesic "Ellipsoidal geodesic") length is often negligible.[[21]](#cite_note-T&G-21)
